ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for white bread with kaya and butter as a composite Singaporean dish; mapped to the nearest bread food product class as a closest_parent anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for kaya toast; A000E (Bread and similar products) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for white bread with kaya and butter as a composite dish.

serving.description: 2 slices of white bread with kaya and butter
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is two slices of white bread (approximately 80 g total) each spread with kaya and a pat of butter, as commonly served at Singaporean kopitiams.

nutrient_source.title: White bread with kaya and butter — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for white bread with kaya and butter (food code F05020128). All per-100g values are as reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for white bread with kaya and butter as a composite dish. GI of 71 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for white bread (wheat flour, average), which is the dominant carbohydrate vehicle. Kaya and butter contribute fat and sugar but do not substantially lower the GI of the white bread base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (71) × available carbohydrate per serving (80 g × 41.69 g/100g = 33.35 g) / 100 = 23.7, rounded to 23.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (320 mg/100g), very low potassium (21 mg/100g), and saturated fat (~8.5 g/100g) from butter and coconut milk in kaya contribute to elevated blood pressure and arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this food due to its unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio and high saturated fat content; if consumed, restrict to a single thin slice and pair with a potassium-rich food such as banana or low-fat milk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 71) white bread base combined with 41.7 g/100g carbohydrate, low dietary fibre (1.96 g/100g), and added sugar from kaya (7.17 g/100g) produces a rapid postprandial glucose spike; saturated fat from butter further worsens insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this food; if consumed, replace white bread with a wholegrain or high-fibre alternative, reduce kaya and butter portions, and pair with a protein source to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~8.5 g/100g) from butter and coconut-milk kaya, trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100g) from butter, cholesterol (53.75 mg/100g), and high sodium (320 mg/100g) collectively raise LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of this food; saturated fat and trans fat content exceed heart-healthy thresholds, and the high glycaemic load further contributes to dyslipidaemia and endothelial dysfunction.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White bread with kaya and butter is a quintessential Singaporean breakfast dish served at kopitiams (traditional coffee shops) and hawker centres across Singapore and Malaysia. It consists of sliced white bread — typically thin-cut, soft sandwich bread or lightly toasted bread — spread generously with kaya (a sweet, fragrant coconut jam made from coconut milk, eggs, sugar, and pandan leaf) and a thick pat of cold salted butter. The combination creates a rich, sweet-savoury flavour profile: the kaya provides a creamy, pandan-scented sweetness while the butter adds a salty, fatty richness that contrasts pleasantly with the soft bread. The dish is traditionally served alongside soft-boiled eggs seasoned with soy sauce and white pepper, and a cup of kopi (local coffee) or teh (local tea). Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 335 kcal, 7.51 g protein, 15.93 g fat (of which approximately 8.5 g is saturated fat from butter and coconut milk), 41.69 g carbohydrate, 7.17 g sugar, 1.96 g dietary fibre, and 320 mg sodium. The high glycaemic index (approximately 71) and low fibre content make this food a high glycaemic load meal that is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without significant portion control and dietary modification. The calcium content (157 mg/100g) is relatively notable, likely contributed by the bread fortification and butter. Iron content is 3.96 mg/100g. This dish is culturally significant as a daily breakfast staple and comfort food in Singapore, Malaysia, and among the Singaporean diaspora worldwide.
common_user_queries: How many calories in white bread with kaya and butter?
Is kaya toast suitable for hypertension?
Is kaya toast high in sugar?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (food code F05020128), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The match is exact. GI is estimated from the white bread analogue in the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source, and the estimation basis is clearly documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05020128 as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data beyond those reported by HPB (potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are not available in the HPB Foodo database entry for this specific composite dish as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g set to 0.1 g/100g: trace trans fat is present from the butter component; this is a clinically relevant amount for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis risk. saturated_fat_g of approximately 8.5 g/100g is estimated from the butter and coconut milk components of kaya as reported in the HPB health suitability notes; the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry, so this value is an informed estimate consistent with the fat composition of butter (~50% saturated) and coconut milk kaya. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate based on white bread (wheat flour) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. Glycemic load per serving calculated for an 80 g serving: GL = 71 × (80 × 0.4169) / 100 = 71 × 33.35 / 100 ≈ 23.7, reported as 23.8.
